feed_card.headline: In most real-world data, the first digit is a 1 about 30% of the time, nearly three times what an even spread would predict.

teasers: Why a leading 1 beats a leading 9 sixfold
The worn page that first gave it away
How auditors spot a cooked ledger

sections.content: In most real-world data, the first digit is a 1 about 30% of the time, nearly three times what an even spread would predict.

sections.content: Ask anyone what fraction of numbers start with a 1 and the honest guess is one in nine, the same as any other digit. There is no obvious reason a river's length or a country's population should favor one opening digit over another. Yet they do, and not by a little. Count the leading digits in almost any large body of real measurements, the populations of cities, the prices on a stock exchange, the lengths of rivers, the readings on a year of electricity bills, and the 1s pile up near thirty percent while the 9s straggle in below five. The numbers are not playing fair, and they break the same way almost everywhere you look.

The pattern shows up the moment you tally real data, which is how Benford came across it. He ran the count across twenty unrelated tables, more than twenty thousand numbers in all, from the surface areas of rivers to physical constants to the street numbers listed in a directory of scientists, and the same lopsided curve came out of each. The reason sits in how we write numbers. Stretch one tenfold step from 1 to 10 along a logarithmic ruler, the kind that spaces 1, 10 and 100 at equal intervals, and the stretch labeled 1 takes up about thirty percent of the length, because climbing from 1 to 2 is a doubling while climbing from 9 to 10 is a change of only a ninth. Any process that scatters its values smoothly across many orders of magnitude lands in that wide 1-zone most often, and in 1995 the mathematician Theodore Hill made the point exact: a distribution that does not depend on the units you measure it in can only be Benford's.

shrink toward 9</text></svg>

content.body: The first clue was a worn book. Around 1881 the astronomer Simon Newcomb noticed that the tables of logarithms he reached for every day fell open at the front, the early pages grimy and soft while the back ones stayed crisp. People were looking up numbers that began with 1 far more than numbers that began with 8 or 9. Newcomb wrote a two-page note giving the right formula and moved on, and the observation sank from view for half a century. In 1938 Frank Benford, a physicist at General Electric, ran into the same uneven wear in his own log tables and this time chased it down, gathering more than twenty thousand numbers from every source he could find before publishing the law that now carries his name. He was not the first to see it. He was the one who showed how far it reached.

key_figures.name: Simon Newcomb
key_figures.one_line: In an 1881 note he traced the unequal wear on his logarithm tables to a real bias toward small leading digits, then set the idea aside.
key_figures.image_attribution: Harris & Ewing, "Simon Newcomb", public domain (Library of Congress via Wikimedia Commons)

key_figures.name: Frank Benford
key_figures.one_line: His 1938 paper counted leading digits across twenty unrelated datasets and showed the bias runs through nature at large, well beyond any single table.

content.title: Cooked books fall off the curve
content.body: Honest ledgers obey the law without trying. Invented ones rarely do, because a person making up figures tends to spread the leading digits too evenly and lean on the middle ones, so the natural glut of 1s never appears. Mark Nigrini turned this into a working audit test in the 1990s, and tax authorities and fraud examiners now run columns of accounts against the Benford curve to see which ones bulge in the wrong place. The technique has been accepted as evidence in United States courts. When economists later checked the deficit figures that European governments had filed before adopting the euro, the data Greece had reported deviated from the law more than any other member state's, the statistical residue of numbers that had been massaged.

content.title: It reaches only a certain kind of number
content.body: The law governs quantities that grow by multiplying and stretch across many sizes, the populations of towns from hamlets to megacities, the lengths of rivers, the prices on a stock exchange, the fundamental constants of physics. It says little about numbers penned into a narrow band or spread evenly by design, the faces of a die, the pockets on a roulette wheel, where one opening digit turns up about as often as the next. Assigned numbers sit outside it too. Phone numbers and lottery draws carry no Benford signature, because nobody measured them into being, somebody chose them.

sections.content: What the law exposes is the shape of how quantities come to be. Things in the world rarely add up in even steps. They multiply, compound and decay, and growth of that kind lays its values down along a logarithmic grain. Our base-ten notation then reads that grain off as a steady surplus of leading 1s. So a column of figures quietly records whether it grew from some real process or was assembled by a hand reaching for plausible digits. That a directory of street numbers, the masses of the planets and a corporation's expense report all bend toward the same curve points to arithmetic itself producing the bend, while rivers and galaxies and ledgers share nothing else between them.

content.question: Why do leading 1s turn up so much more often than leading 9s in real-world data?
options: Most instruments round their readings down toward 1
People prefer to write down numbers that start with 1
On a logarithmic scale the band that begins with 1 is the widest part of every tenfold step
There are more whole numbers starting with 1 than with 9
content.explanation: Values spread across many orders of magnitude fall most often into the widest part of each tenfold step, and the span from 1 to 2 covers about 30 percent of that step while 9 to 10 covers under 5.

content.question: Which of these datasets should follow Benford's law most closely?
options: The heights of adult men in centimeters
The numbers drawn in a national lottery
The faces showing on thrown dice
The populations of the world's cities
content.explanation: The law fits quantities that span many orders of magnitude and arise by multiplying. City populations run from a few hundred to tens of millions, while dice and lottery draws are bounded or evenly designed and carry no such bias.

content.question: An auditor finds a firm's expense figures have far too few leading 1s and a bulge of 6s and 7s. What does Benford's law license them to conclude?
options: The books are certainly fraudulent
The figures deserve a closer look, since honest data rarely departs this far
The firm is larger than it reports
The accountant must have made rounding errors
content.explanation: A departure from the curve only marks data for a closer look. Legitimate data can deviate for ordinary reasons, so the test points investigators toward the columns worth examining and proves nothing by itself.

content.question: Why does converting a dataset from dollars to euros leave its Benford pattern intact?
options: The exchange rate happens to preserve first digits
Currencies are all built on powers of ten
The euro and dollar are close enough in value
Benford's is the one leading-digit distribution that survives any change of scale
content.explanation: Converting currency just multiplies every value by a fixed factor, a change of units, and Hill showed the only leading-digit distribution untouched by such a change is Benford's. A dataset that obeys it in one currency obeys it in all.

content.question: Roughly what share of values in a Benford dataset open with either a 1 or a 2?
options: About one in five
About a third
Nearly half
About seven in ten
content.explanation: The first two digits alone take about 30 and 18 percent, so close to half of all values begin with a 1 or a 2, while the four highest digits together account for only about a fifth.

content.question: Why does fabricated data tend to fail a Benford test?
options: People inventing figures spread the leading digits too evenly and lean on the middle ones
Fraudsters always reach for round numbers
Computers generate digits at random
Made-up numbers are usually too large
content.explanation: Someone reaching for plausible numbers rarely reproduces the natural surplus of leading 1s, so invented columns come out flatter and heavier in the middle digits than real measurement ever would.

content.myth: Any list of numbers should follow Benford's law.
content.reality: Only data that spans many orders of magnitude and arises from measurement obeys it. Bounded ranges, evenly designed numbers and assigned codes like phone numbers do not.

content.myth: If a dataset breaks the law, the numbers were faked.
content.reality: A departure only marks data worth a second look. Honest figures deviate for ordinary reasons all the time, so the test points investigators to where they should check.

content.body: The law is on solid ground as a description, and Hill's invariance result explains why anything indifferent to its units must follow it. What stays unsettled is the deeper why: which real processes in the world produce Benford behavior, and why so many unrelated ones do. Multiplication, the mixing together of many different distributions, and repeated random growth have each been shown to generate it, yet no single mechanism covers every case, and there is no clean test that says in advance whether a given dataset will comply.
items: Is there one underlying reason so many natural datasets follow the law, or several unrelated ones that happen to converge on it?
Can the conditions a dataset must meet be stated sharply enough to predict compliance before the digits are counted?

content.label: Benford's law. Wikipedia (digit percentages, Newcomb and Benford history, Hill's scale-invariance result, Greek data, US court admissibility)
Newcomb S. Note on the Frequency of Use of the Different Digits in Natural Numbers. American Journal of Mathematics 1881;4:39-40 (scanned original)
Benford F. The Law of Anomalous Numbers. Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society 1938;78:551-572
Hill TP. A Statistical Derivation of the Significant-Digit Law. Statistical Science 1995;10(4):354-363
Rauch B, Gottsche M, Brahler G, Engel S. Fact and Fiction in EU-Governmental Economic Data. German Economic Review 2011;12(3):243-255 (Greece's reported data deviated most from Benford's law)
Using Excel and Benford's Law to detect fraud. Journal of Accountancy 2017 (forensic accounting application popularized by Mark Nigrini)
What Is Benford's Law? Why This Unexpected Pattern of Numbers Is Everywhere. Scientific American 2023
